narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path despite the challenges many face, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admission system uses a model trained on data from thousands of students like you, looking at factors such as undergraduate grades, LSAT scores, early and later school performance deciles, family income, and whether you'd study full-time to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you specifically, with your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 33, Year 1 and Year 3 deciles both at 3, upper-middle family income, and full-time study plans, the model estimated a 44.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam—high enough to trigger the rejection, as the school aims to admit those predicted to pass with strong confidence. Your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive influence overall, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass despite being below the typical 3.21 average. Similarly, your LSAT score, though under the dataset's 36.70 average, also worked in your favor by suggesting a likelihood of success. On the flip side, your Year 3 decile of 3—below the average of 5.53—was the top factor nudging the model toward predicting failure, indicating performance lagging behind many peers. Your Year 1 decile of 3 offered some counterbalance by leaning toward success, while your full-time status and upper-middle income provided minor boosts in the same direction. Compared to typical students, your academics are in the lower third across key metrics, which amplified the concerns despite those helpful elements. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—it's just how these patterns played out for you here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above average, strengthening your later-year performance through targeted study habits, and highlighting any unique experiences or improvements in a reapplication or appeal. Many have turned similar profiles around with focused effort, and resources tailored for women and Black applicants, like prep programs or mentors, could make a real difference. You're capable of rewriting this story—keep pushing.
